beats.text: A language model predicts the next token, but an agent acting in the world needs to predict the next state instead.

visual.sub: A learned simulator of an environment's dynamics

beats.text: A world model encodes an observation into a compact latent state, then predicts the next latent and reward from the current state and action.
Predicting every pixel wastes capacity on textures and shadows that do not matter, so dynamics are learned in a low-dimensional latent space instead.
Feeding each predicted latent back into the transition rolls the model forward in imagination, generating futures without touching the real environment.
A world model can do three jobs: predict next states, generate whole futures, and plan by scoring imagined action sequences.
Ha and Schmidhuber showed in 2018 an agent trained entirely inside its own dream of a racing game, then transferred to the real one.
Dreamer is the most complete worked example of latent-dynamics control, built around a Recurrent State-Space Model.

visual.sub: DreamerV1 through V3, Hafner et al.

beats.text: The latent splits into a deterministic recurrent state that carries history and a stochastic state that represents genuine uncertainty.

visual.note: The recurrent hidden state carries history forward

beats.text: Training pulls the prior, which sees only the recurrent state, toward the posterior, which sees the real observation, via a KL term.

visual.note: EQ MM5.2 — prior must anticipate what the posterior knows

beats.text: Dreamer trains an actor and critic purely on imagined rollouts of about fifteen steps, never planning in the real environment.
Because a latent forward pass is far cheaper than a real simulator step, Dreamer can practise millions of imagined steps per real step.
DreamerV3 used one fixed hyperparameter set across Atari, control and DMLab, and was first to mine diamonds in Minecraft from scratch.
LeCun's 2022 position paper makes one commitment central: do not predict in observation space, predict in representation space.
A predictor maps the context embedding to the target embedding, with stop-gradient and an EMA target encoder to avoid collapse.

visual.note: EQ MM5.3 — predict the embedding, not the pixels

beats.text: By predicting an embedding, JEPA can discard unpredictable detail like textures, keeping only what is predictable and dropping noise.
I-JEPA on images and V-JEPA on video learned features competitive with reconstruction methods while training faster without heavy augmentation.
Collapse remains a finicky failure mode, so EMA targets, stop-gradient, and VICReg variance regularisers are load-bearing, not optional.
Without a decoder you cannot visualise a JEPA prediction, so it trades interpretability for representational efficiency.

visual.label: A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence

beats.text: Genie pushes the world model toward generating playable worlds, producing an environment you can step through from a single prompt.
It was trained on over 200,000 hours of internet platformer gameplay video, with no action labels at all.
The latent action module infers, for each pair of frames, a discrete action from a tiny codebook that best explains the transition.

visual.note: EQ MM5.4 — latent action inferred, not labelled

beats.text: Because the codebook holds only eight actions, it is forced to capture controllable recurring moves like jump and move rather than memorise pixels.
Genie loosens the binding constraint on agent training, since passive video is essentially unlimited while action-labelled data is scarce.
These simulators still hallucinate, drift over long horizons, and approximate physics, so they are research artifacts not physics engines.
A world model turns reinforcement learning from a problem of acting into a problem of imagining, spent in two dominant ways.
Background planning trains a fast reactive policy on imagined rollouts, then acts with the policy alone at deployment.
Decision-time planning chooses the action sequence whose imagined discounted return is greatest, then executes only the first action.

visual.note: EQ MM5.5 — planning as imagined-return maximisation

beats.text: MuZero learns the transition, reward and value, then runs tree search over them, mastering Go, chess and Atari without being given the rules.
Compounding error is the recurring tension: each small error feeds the next step, so an H-step rollout can be useless even with good one-step predictions.
Search amplifies model error, so background planning that only needs local accuracy is often more robust than long decision-time search.
